lessons.title: Cell Signalling and the Cell Cycle

content.body: Cells communicate through chemical signals. The cell cycle controls how cells grow and divide. Errors in this cycle can lead to cancer.

Cell Cycle Phases:
G1 → S → G2 → M (Mitosis)

G1: Growth and protein synthesis
S:  DNA replication
G2: Preparation for division
M:  Actual cell division (PMAT: Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase)

content.title: Mitosis Stages Mnemonic
content.body:
PMAT helps remember mitosis:
Prophase: chromosomes condense, spindle forms
Metaphase: chromosomes align at equator
Anaphase: chromatids pulled to poles
Telophase: nuclear envelopes reform, cells divide

questions.question: During which phase of the cell cycle does DNA replication occur?
questions.explanation: S = Synthesis phase. DNA is copied during this phase

questions.question: Chromosomes line up at the cell equator during:
questions.explanation: In Metaphase, chromosomes align at the metaphase plate (equator)

questions.question: Sister chromatids are separated in:
questions.explanation: Spindle fibres pull sister chromatids to opposite poles in Anaphase

questions.question: A proto-oncogene becomes dangerous when it mutates into a/an:
questions.explanation: Mutated proto-oncogenes become oncogenes that drive uncontrolled cell division

questions.question: Mitosis produces:
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces 2 genetically identical daughter cells, each diploid (2n)

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

content.body: Cells communicate with each other through chemical signals. This is essential for coordinating activities in multicellular organisms, from the immune response to development and growth.

content.title: Types of Cell Signalling
content.body:
Endocrine signalling: hormones secreted into blood, travel long distances (e.g. insulin, adrenaline)
Paracrine signalling: signal acts on nearby cells (e.g. prostaglandins, growth factors)
Autocrine signalling: cell signals itself
Synaptic signalling: neurotransmitters across synaptic cleft (short range, fast)

Signal transduction:
1. Reception: signal molecule (ligand) binds to receptor protein
2. Transduction: cascade of molecular changes (second messengers like cAMP)
3. Response: change in cell activity (enzyme activation, gene expression)

Receptors can be on cell surface (for water-soluble signals) or inside cell (for lipid-soluble signals like steroid hormones)

questions.question: Insulin is an example of...
questions.explanation: Insulin is secreted by pancreatic β cells into the blood and travels to target cells throughout the body.

questions.question: cAMP (cyclic AMP) acts as a...
questions.explanation: cAMP is a second messenger — it amplifies and transmits the signal inside the cell after the receptor is activated.

questions.question: Steroid hormone receptors are located...
questions.explanation: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and can diffuse through the membrane. Their receptors are inside the cell.

questions.question: Signal amplification in a cascade means...
options: Each step produces many more molecules
questions.answer: Each step produces many more molecules
questions.explanation: In a signalling cascade, each activated molecule activates many others, greatly amplifying a small original signal.

questions.question: Neurotransmitters use which type of signalling?
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are released across a synapse to signal to an adjacent neuron or muscle cell — synaptic signalling.

questions.question: A receptor tyrosine kinase is activated by...
questions.explanation: RTKs are membrane receptors that phosphorylate themselves and substrates when a growth factor binds.

questions.question: Which signalling molecule can pass through the cell membrane?
questions.explanation: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and diffuse through the phospholipid bilayer directly to intracellular receptors.

questions.question: Signal transduction converts...
questions.explanation: Signal transduction: the process of converting an external chemical signal into specific intracellular events.

questions.question: Paracrine signalling affects...
questions.explanation: Paracrine signals act locally on nearby cells — they diffuse short distances and are rapidly degraded.

questions.question: G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) work by...
options: Activating G-proteins that activate adenylyl cyclase to make cAMP
questions.answer: Activating G-proteins that activate adenylyl cyclase to make cAMP
questions.explanation: GPCR activation → G-protein activates adenylyl cyclase → ATP converted to cAMP (second messenger) → cellular response.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Cell fractionation separates cellular components so they can be studied individually. Ultracentrifugation separates organelles by size and density.

Steps:
1. Homogenise tissue in cold, isotonic buffer (pH 7)
   Cold: prevents enzyme activity
   Isotonic: prevents osmotic damage
   pH 7: prevents denaturation
2. Filter to remove debris
3. Centrifuge at increasing speeds:
   Low speed (~1000g): nuclei, cell debris (largest)
   Medium (~10,000g): mitochondria, chloroplasts
   High (~100,000g): ribosomes, ER, membranes

content.title: Organelle Functions Summary
content.body:
Nucleus: houses DNA, controls cell activities, site of transcription
Mitochondria: ATP production by aerobic respiration
Chloroplast: photosynthesis (in plant/algal cells)
Ribosomes: protein synthesis (translation)
Rough ER: protein processing and transport
Smooth ER: lipid synthesis
Golgi apparatus: modifies, packages and ships proteins
Lysosomes: intracellular digestion (contain hydrolytic enzymes)
Vacuole: storage, turgor pressure (plants)
Centrioles: form spindle during cell division (animal cells)

content.title: Electron Microscopy
content.body:
TEM (Transmission Electron Microscope):
- Electrons pass through ultra-thin specimen
- Very high resolution (0.2nm), can see organelles
- Must be dead/fixed specimens

SEM (Scanning Electron Microscope):
- Electrons scan surface
- 3D surface images
- Lower resolution than TEM
- Also dead specimens

questions.question: Why is isotonic buffer used in cell fractionation?
options: To prevent osmotic damage to organelles
questions.answer: To prevent osmotic damage to organelles
questions.explanation: Isotonic solution has same water potential as cells — prevents organelles from swelling (in hypotonic) or shrinking (hypertonic).

questions.question: Nuclei are separated in cell fractionation at
questions.explanation: Nuclei are the largest, heaviest structures — they pellet first at low centrifugal force (~1000g).

questions.question: Ribosomes are collected at
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are tiny — require very high centrifugal force (~100,000g) to pellet.

questions.question: The Golgi apparatus's main role is
options: modifying, packaging, and shipping proteins from the ER
questions.answer: modifying, packaging, and shipping proteins from the ER
questions.explanation: Golgi receives vesicles from rough ER, modifies proteins (glycosylation), sorts and ships them to correct destinations.

questions.question: Lysosomes contain
questions.explanation: Lysosomes are membrane-bound vesicles packed with digestive enzymes — digest worn-out organelles and ingested material.

questions.question: TEM can resolve structures as small as
questions.explanation: Transmission electron microscope resolution: ~0.2nm (200 picometres) — can see individual large molecules.

questions.question: Why must specimens for electron microscopy be dead?
options: The vacuum and preparation procedures (fixing, staining) are incompatible with life
questions.answer: The vacuum and preparation procedures (fixing, staining) are incompatible with life
questions.explanation: EM requires vacuum chamber (electrons scatter in air) and chemical fixation/staining processes — incompatible with life.

questions.question: Centrioles are absent in
questions.explanation: Most plant cells lack centrioles (and asters). Instead, the preprophase band and phragmoplast coordinate cell division.

questions.question: Smooth ER differs from rough ER by
options: lacking ribosomes and specialising in lipid synthesis
questions.answer: lacking ribosomes and specialising in lipid synthesis
questions.explanation: Smooth ER: no ribosomes. Functions: lipid and steroid synthesis, drug detoxification (liver), Ca²⁺ storage (muscle).

questions.question: Which organelle has a double membrane with cristae?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria have outer and inner membranes. Inner membrane folds into cristae — greatly increasing surface area for ATP synthesis.

questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Advanced Cell Biology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Advanced Cell Biology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Advanced Cell Biology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Advanced Cell Biology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Advanced Cell Biology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Advanced Cell Biology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Advanced Cell Biology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
